title: From a View to a Kill: Drones and Late Modern War
author: Gregory

opening_move.description: The dominant critique of drone warfare claims it reduces killing to a video game through physical distance and emotional detachment. Gregory inverts this: the real problem is not remoteness but intimacy — the visual technologies of UAVs produce a constructed proximity that privileges the hunter-killer's perspective and makes killing more, not less, deadly and morally distorted.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The 'video game war' critique misidentifies the moral problem with drones. The constructed visibility of drone warfare — the techno-cultural production of proximity through full-motion video — creates forms of intimacy and interpellation that have profound implications for targeting ethics, civilian casualties, and the conduct of late modern war.

rivals_engaged: 'video game war' critique (remote killing as detachment)
virtuous war discourse (precision strikes as ethical)
theoretical_positioning.description: Gregory argues against both the military's self-presentation (virtuous, surgical precision) and the critics' counter-claim (video game detachment). His intervention is that drone warfare produces a special kind of techno-cultural intimacy through scopic regimes — constructed visibility that is simultaneously a constructed invisibility — which is more dangerous and ethically troubling than distance.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Analysis of military documents, pilot testimonies, combat footage descriptions, and technology specifications (Predator/Reaper sensor systems, Gorgon Stare, ARGUS-IS); historical comparison with British aerial counterinsurgency in Afghanistan/Iraq (1920s); analysis of CIA drone strike data in Pakistan and USAF operations in Afghanistan.
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Cultural geography and critical military studies; scopic regimes analysis (visual culture); colonial genealogy of aerial counterinsurgency; interpellation theory applied to video feed experience.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper is a normative-interpretive analysis of drone warfare using rich qualitative and documentary evidence. The scopic regime concept (constructed visibility/invisibility) is the key analytical framework. The empirical material (pilot testimony, system specifications) is constitutive of the argument about techno-cultural intimacy.

case_type.case_name: US drone warfare (CIA Pakistan / USAF Afghanistan campaigns)

normative_stake.domain: critical military studies, political geography, ethics of violence

confidence_notes: This paper has a high normative stake: it makes a substantive argument about how drone warfare should be understood morally and politically, against both military apologetics and the dominant critical framing. The counter_objection_response move is explicit (section 'Video Game War?'). The genealogical move traces aerial counterinsurgency from 1920s British colonial practices to present. The constitutive evidence includes pilot testimonies, technical specifications, and military doctrine.
